items.front: When must en route weather be transmitted to an arriving aircraft before or as part of the approach clearance?
items.back: When official weather is below the stated ceiling or visibility thresholds and the report is being delivered directly or through an authorized relay path.

items.front: What relay method is authorized when the weather report is not transmitted directly to the pilot?
items.back: A communications station other than an air carrier company radio, or a nonapproach control facility directed to issue current weather.

items.front: May a nonapproach control facility be instructed to issue current weather to satisfy this rule?
items.back: Yes.

items.front: What kind of weather changes are also transmitted under this rule?
items.back: Changes classified as special weather observations.

items.front: If an arriving aircraft reports weather below the pilot's landing minima, what should ATC do for that aircraft?
items.back: Issue appropriate instructions to hold or proceed to another airport.

items.front: What must ATC do with other aircraft desiring approaches after a below-minima report?
items.back: Adjust their position in the landing sequence as necessary and issue approach clearances accordingly.

items.front: Who determines whether the existing weather or visibility is adequate for the approach and landing?
items.back: The pilot or aircraft operator.

items.front: Does a below-minima pilot report automatically prohibit all remaining approaches?
items.back: No. ATC adjusts the sequence and clearances as necessary.

items.front: When is approach information provided by en route and terminal approach control sectors?
items.back: On initial contact or as soon as possible thereafter.

items.front: When may ATIS approach information be omitted?
items.back: When the pilot states the appropriate ATIS code.

items.front: At an airport without ATIS, after the pilot advises receipt of automated weather, which items may be omitted?
items.back: Surface wind, threshold-triggered ceiling and visibility, and altimeter setting.

items.front: What weather-related items are part of the normal approach-information package when they are not omitted?
items.back: Surface wind, threshold-triggered ceiling and visibility, and the altimeter setting.

items.front: What must controllers provide upon pilot request regarding automated weather?
items.back: The frequency where automated weather may be obtained and, if appropriate, that airport weather is not available.

items.front: If the pilot cannot receive the ASOS or AWOS broadcast, what must ATC issue?
items.back: The last long-line disseminated weather.

items.front: What phraseology is used for the automated weather frequency advisory?
items.back: (Airport) AWOS/ASOS weather available on (frequency).

items.front: When need special weather observations no longer be issued separately?
items.back: After they are included in ATIS and the pilot states the appropriate ATIS code.

items.front: When tower and TRACON are part of the same facility, what arrival information is forwarded to the tower?
items.back: Aircraft identification, type if needed for separation, and the approach procedure and or runway if different from that in use.

items.front: What is one item approach control forwards to centers about holding operations?
items.back: The highest altitude being used by approach control at the holding fix.

items.front: When must a revised EFC be forwarded to center?
items.back: When it differs by 10 minutes or more from the one issued by center.

items.front: When may STARS modify or quick look functions be used to forward arrival data?
items.back: When the tower has STARS data on its CTRD and a facility directive or letter of agreement outlines the transfer procedures.

items.front: Who determines whether conditions are adequate for using STARS arrival data on the CTRD where authorized?
items.back: The local controller.

items.front: Before issuing an approach clearance, what destination information must be provided when known?
items.back: Abnormal operation of approach or landing aids and destination airport conditions that might restrict an approach or landing.

items.front: When may that airport-condition information be omitted?
items.back: When it is contained in ATIS and the pilot states the appropriate ATIS code.

items.front: Which en route airport-condition items are specifically excluded from the package?
items.back: Taxiway, apron, or service-area NOTAMs outside the landing area, plus other listed exclusions such as obstruction information for instrument approaches and cold-temperature compensation information.

items.front: When may RwyCC from a FICON NOTAM be issued?
items.back: Before or with approach clearance, before en route descent clearance, prior to departure in terminal operations, and as soon as possible after later changes.

items.front: May RwyCC be issued instead of the full FICON NOTAM?
items.back: Yes. The full FICON NOTAM is issued on pilot request if workload permits.

items.front: Does a pilot saying, "We have the NOTAMs," relieve ATC of the duty to issue restricting airport conditions?
items.back: No.

items.front: When must facilities coordinate during an ILS runway switch at an airport with multiple systems not used simultaneously?
items.back: When changing from one ILS to another under those conditions.

items.front: With whom must the ILS switch be coordinated?
items.back: Facilities that use fixes formed by reference to the affected NAVAIDs.

items.front: Why is this coordination required?
items.back: Because other facilities are using fixes formed by reference to the NAVAIDs being switched.

items.front: When should the coordination occur relative to the switch?
items.back: Before making the switch.
